notes: Figure gathered from the practice’s public information. Confirm current pricing and exactly what it includes directly with ESSI.
Multidisciplinary endometriosis institute. Currency (USD vs. EUR) and what the figure includes not confirmed; check with the clinic.
Shared without further detail.

The anesthesiologist turned out to be out-of-network. Patient disputed the claims and the balance fully cleared after about a year; she described the outstanding debt as taking a serious mental-health toll until it did. In the end she paid only her expected copays.
Shared without further detail.
Stage IV, about 3 hours in the OR. A one-year follow-up transvaginal ultrasound was later billed to insurance at ≈ $10,000 (patient’s copay: $15), a red flag she noted for future planning.

Stage IV, described as a very hard case: 4-hour surgery, 4 nights in hospital. Insurance covered 100% beyond the (high) deductible; surgeon’s office and insurer went back and forth on payment for almost a year.
Patient had already hit her out-of-pocket maximum for the year, which capped her share.
Running total across her first endometriosis surgery; no breakdown shared.
High-deductible plan with a $6,500 out-of-pocket maximum at the time.
Reports never paying anything out of pocket for three endometriosis surgeries in the US.

US patient living in the UK. A complex multidisciplinary case (endo specialist, urologist and bowel specialist) with a few days’ inpatient stay, scheduled about a month out at the time of the report. The private hospital requires full payment up front; she’ll file an insurance claim for reimbursement afterward and is considering the hospital’s financing plan for part of it. (Two comments from this patient are combined here.)
Remarkably fast same-day care: arrived 14:30, wheeled into surgery at 15:00, out and heading home by 19:00.
Quote covers the pre-surgery ultrasound, the surgery itself, and overnight monitoring.
Patient flew from Canada for surgery. Figure is the surgeon’s fee; facility and anesthesia costs weren’t specified and are typically billed separately.
Paid entirely out of pocket with no insurance coverage.
Estimate received before her excision surgery: insurance is set to cover $123,304.73 of the total, leaving roughly $6.5k to her.
Hysterectomy with removal of endo and cysts, though the endo was ablated rather than excised, and she suspects regrowth.

provider_detail: 1st surgery, same patient as next row
notes: Her first surgery, with strong insurance benefits, which she notes counts as cheap where she lives.

provider_detail: 2nd surgery, same patient as prior row
notes: Hospital system wasn’t covered by her insurance. She was supposed to stay overnight, didn’t, and got a separate ED bill for a catheter. The billing office then wrongly attached her insurance (which would have voided the self-pay discount and pushed the bill past $40k) and it took nearly a month of daily calls, while very sick, to get it removed.
Sent to collections and still paying the balances six years later, with another surgery now needed.
Surgery with a specialist; she calls the pre-insurance price insane.
Her surgery was free; she notes private-clinic endo surgery in Lithuania starts around €2,000 and suggests traveling there could beat many quotes.
Deductible already met, so the plan covered 100% of the hospital charges except the surgeon’s $15k fee, billed separately. She calls the whole thing hugely educational about how insurance works.
Paid out of pocket and hoping for money back; the surgery saved her ovaries.
Included a 4-night hospital stay and both an endometriosis surgeon and a colorectal surgeon. She had already hit her deductible and out-of-pocket max, so she paid nothing.
Public surgery is free in Ireland but the waiting list runs about 6 years; her private insurance got her a surgery date in roughly 6 weeks.
Shared without further detail.
Deductible was already met through her MRI and appointments; after surgery the insurer refused to cover about $700 of items.
Two surgeries, both free on the NHS.
Estimate received the week before posting; surgery not yet done.
All four of her surgeries were covered under OHIP.
Five excision surgeries plus a complete hysterectomy; the only out-of-pocket costs were parking fees and her partner’s waiting-room snacks.
Done at a private hospital; her only cost was the policy excess.
Shared without further detail.
Struggled for a decade; reports private surgery runs about AU$10,000 and credits finding her gynaecologist with making care possible without insurance.

provider_detail: Center for Endometriosis and Advanced Pelvic Surgery
notes: Internationally known specialist in neuro-gynecologic surgery. Her earlier diagnostic lap had been 100% covered because its primary purpose was sterilization.
Paid only her deductible and co-insurance, which she notes still hurt.
A long procedure billed at only $10k, on the very low end of US insurer billing in this tracker.

paid_display: covered by partner’s plan, amount not shared
paid_note: covered by partner’s plan, amount not shared
notes: Stage IV: a 4-hour surgery with three surgeons and a 4-night hospital stay. Details closely mirror another $1M stage-IV report in this tracker; possibly the same surgery shared in two places.
First: open myomectomy and cystectomy. Second: laparoscopic myomectomy, cystectomy and adhesiolysis. She finds even these amounts a lot, and US figures saddening.
Excision with a hysterectomy in the same operation; she paid only what was left of her yearly out-of-pocket maximum.
All-in price at a private hospital, including the overnight stay.
Her surgery was free; even the uninsured price would have been under €3,000. US figures feel unreal to her.
Paid nothing and never saw a cost; expects another surgery within 1-2 years.
Paid roughly 7k with an endometriosis specialist in Greece; finds US pricing baffling.
Could have used the NHS for free but the waiting lists are long, so she went private through her workplace medical insurance.
Paid nothing for the surgery itself, just the standard €10/day hospital fee. She notes the wide cost range reflects how much surgery duration varies.
Just a $50 copay; she never even looked at the underlying bill.

charges_note: cash price · surgeon takes no insurance
notes: Flat fee quoted by an excision specialist who doesn’t accept insurance.
Surgery fully covered; her only cost was about $25 of medication afterward.
Charge covered excision plus a sterilization procedure done in the same operation.

Insurance covered ~$100k, but the specialist didn’t work with her plan, leaving ~$30k to her at age 28. She didn’t qualify for financial help due to income; the practice offered a very reasonable payment plan. She now lives somewhere with far cheaper, covered healthcare.
Had already hit her deductible, so she owed nothing.

She only knew about the $8k surgeon fee and had saved for it. The week of surgery, the hospital said it needed $47,000 on the day and refused service without it, forcing her to cancel. The surgeon called the pricing typical; she was stunned.
Her only out-of-pocket cost at a private hospital was the anaesthesiologist.
Counts herself very lucky at about AU$1,500 out of pocket.
Still waiting on the actual bill at the time of the report.
Fully covered under Tricare Prime (US military coverage).
Managed a self-pay excision in the US for under $7k, proof the giant quotes aren’t universal.

Her insurance was billed incorrectly and she was pursued for $1.5M. About a year after the procedure she finally settled it down to a few thousand dollars, and described living with intense anxiety the whole time the seven-figure balance hung over her.
A very lengthy Stage IV excision surgery.
Shared without further detail.
Full hysterectomy with a major bowel resection near the rectum and a temporary ileostomy: endo specialist, urologist and bowel surgeon, with a 5-day stay.

Exploratory laparoscopy, no bowel involvement. She describes Belgian care as extremely cost-efficient (she hopped onto the surgical bed herself) but excellent and inexpensive, and offered to share hospital details.
Three-day hospital stay including a hysterectomy and extensive bowel excision, €10,000 total.
Reported typical UK private pricing, varying with how extensive the surgery is and whether a colorectal surgeon is needed.

Many quotes covered only an exploratory laparoscopy with biopsies; anything complicated would be left for another specialist. Most surgeons were out-of-network with separate surgeon, hospital and anesthesia fees; her insurance would cover only a minimum, and stays over 24h add anesthesia staff fees.
Hysterectomy, oophorectomy and endo excision with a primary endo surgeon plus a bowel surgeon, and a 3-night private-hospital stay.

charges_val: total system cost unknown to patient
charges_note: total system cost unknown to patient
notes: Paid €1,500 out of pocket, half of which her private mutuelle refunded. Credits the French healthcare system.
Other medical bills earlier in the year had already covered her deductible, so last week’s surgery was essentially free.
Paid amounts up to her deductible from savings. Her advice: audit your bills while claims are still processing, since you can be overcharged before deductible-qualifying claims post.

Two surgeries, each just a $90 copay; one included a full hysterectomy. She notes Dr. Kossl has since moved toward a fibroid team, but recommends consulting NYU’s endo specialists; NYU took her insurance with surgeon fees completely covered.
Also went through NYU with a different surgeon and paid only the copay.
Shared without further detail.
Counts herself lucky: a $10 copay for her 2024 surgery.

paid_note: to OON surgeon (incl. consult) + plan out-of-pocket max
notes: Complex case needing a bowel resection; her in-network gynecological surgeon wasn’t comfortable operating. The well-regarded endo specialist was out-of-network (~$10k in fees), but the hospital and colorectal surgeon were in-network, so the rest capped at her out-of-pocket max.

paid_display: insurance covered most of the bill
paid_note: insurance covered most of the bill
notes: Quoted $5k with Dr. Fogelson; insurance ended up covering most of it. She notes how wild some other estimates have gotten.

Her first diagnostic/excision surgery ran about $4,000 with insurance; the second is quoted at $6,200 and on hold while she weighs CareCredit-style financing and Texas financial-aid programs, which she says involve a lot of hoops. Also pays a $75 specialist copay versus $35 for a GP. (Several comments from this patient combined.)

Her original plan (Sutter Health) allowed no out-of-network or out-of-state care, so she switched to Anthem Blue Cross to access the practice. Six months post-op with no pain; she says it was worth every penny.
Same insurer as the other Portland reports: quoted just $680 out of pocket.

paid_note: insurer paid half · surgeon waived the rest
notes: Excision, hysterectomy, left ovary removal, appendectomy and prolapse repair. Insurance covered half; her surgeon told her not to react to the bill and waived the remainder. Her takeaway: a surgeon’s kindness can change everything.
The practice let her pay in installments. She adds that Medicaid, for those who qualify, makes surgery free, and that traveling can get you a better quote.

paid_note: balance owed, on a payment plan
notes: Insurance paid a good portion; she’s repaying the remaining ~$6k through a payment plan.
Paid about $10k on top of what insurance covered, using money she had saved for her wedding; her fiancé called off the engagement after her diagnosis.
Chose Dr. Mohling after hearing of people being charged far more by another well-known specialist she had considered, and found her excellent.

No insurance for either excision. The first came through an ER visit billed about $50k all-in; the hospital's financial aid and sliding scale brought it to roughly $5k. The aid extended to her second excision six months later, fully covered apart from $600 of blood tests done outside the hospital.
Free, but she waited years and had to fight hard to get the surgery.
Stayed in-network on a federal BCBS plan; her whole share came to about $500.
Quote range just received; surgery still ahead of her.

A 2010 tubal ligation during which her surgeon ablated all the endo she found; that operation produced her first endo diagnosis, at 37, after years of dismissal. She doubts insurance would have approved surgery for endo alone with no prior diagnosis, and notes the hospital has since shut down. An older data point, kept for contrast with today's billing.
Still a financial stretch for her, but she needed the diagnosis to keep accessing treatment.
Surgery three weeks before the report, with a surgeon she calls an expert: worth every penny to her.
Couldn't afford the $10k specialist fee, then learned up to $300k more would come from the hospital itself, which doesn't accept her insurance. She calls it unreal.
Grateful it landed there, because she badly needed the excision.
Went into debt and borrowed what she could because she needed the surgery badly; far from ideal, but there wasn't much choice.

provider: The Endometriosis Clinic · Mr. Peter Barton-Smith
notes: Patient reports a very positive surgical experience and highlights that the clinic posts its prices publicly, a rarity in this space.

Hysterectomy with suspected bowel involvement; the top of the range applies if a resection is needed and includes a 5-day stay. Chose Turkey partly for her partner's family support there, but says the doctors she spoke with might have made it her pick regardless.

The practice lowered the fee to her out-of-network out-of-pocket max and made sure the hospital, anesthesiologist and biopsy were all in-network. A pelvic MRI confirming deep infiltrating endo settled her decision; an in-network DC-area specialist she called in March had no consult opening until August. She found him deeply knowledgeable and validating.

provider_detail: Center for Endometriosis and Advanced Pelvic Surgery
notes: Two surgeries at about $15k each. Felt genuinely listened to and appreciated how quickly surgery could be scheduled; expensive, but in her view a very skilled and compassionate surgeon.

provider_detail: Center for Endometriosis and Advanced Pelvic Surgery
notes: Considered CareCredit-style financing before paying by credit card. Praises his skill (her excision scars are barely noticeable), though she felt less looked-after post-op than at consult, possibly holiday-season timing.
Also points low-income neighbors to Harris County's Gold Card program (county specialists and facilities required) and to Medicaid for Women in Texas.

Her plan covers 80%; the ultrasound consult alone ran over $400 out of pocket. She's still paying off her last cyst-removal surgery while scheduling the next, after regrowth and returning symptoms within a year of the first.
Needed only two pre-surgery appointments (a PA, then her specialist). Would do it again for her health, and suggests gathering estimates before booking, then saving up.

About four hours in hospital for excision from her bowel, ureters and throughout the pelvis, plus removal of an endometrioma and her appendix. She was shocked by the surgical report's extent given her mild symptoms and suspects inflation. Cigna denied the resubmitted $320k claim as final; an external appeals agency is fighting it, and she describes the situation as deeply distressing. (Two comments combined.)
January exploratory lap: a look around with no biopsies taken, billed at $30k.
January surgery: fulguration of lesions on the ovary, pelvic viscera and peritoneal lining.

Her insurer confirmed coverage beforehand from the surgeon's estimate. The same surgeon and surgery center's cash price is $15,000, versus $400k billed through insurance; she suspects billed figures are largely negotiating positions between hospitals and insurers, with patients caught in the middle.
Full breakdown estimate for a robotic laparoscopic myomectomy (multiple fibroids) plus an exploratory lap with excision if endo is found; covers physician, anesthesiologist and hospital fees.
Shared without further detail.

Reported by her mother: the pre-surgery cost estimate was spot-on, and the insurer's contracted rates with San Francisco hospitals mean it likely paid less than the billed amount. Her advice: get pre-approval, and let the insurer do the negotiating rather than you.

paid_note: entire trip: surgery, MRI and tests, 11 nights, flights, food
notes: Traveled from elsewhere in Europe for surgery in Romania. The whole 11-day trip, including the operation, an MRI and other tests, an Airbnb, plane tickets and food, came to about $6,000; she met US patients there who found traveling abroad cheaper than surgery at home.

paid_note: ≈ $7k surgeon (incl. pre/post-op visits and imaging) + in-network hospital
notes: Oregon patient, surgery in October. The surgeon's ~$7k fee under out-of-network benefits included pre- and post-op visits and imaging; hospital costs were in-network and her out-of-pocket max was already met. She also lost almost $18k of income that year from being too sick to work, a cost no bill captures, and credits pelvic-floor physical therapy for her recovery.
Shared without further detail.

paid_note: each, across 6 laparoscopies · $220 specialist consult copay
notes: Six laps, all in that range because her surgeon operates at an ambulatory surgery center instead of a hospital, which she notes is far cheaper. Payment was required up front, so she used CareCredit.

charges_note: without insurance, per prices she has seen
paid_note: insurer covered about 80%; exact share not stated
notes: Coverage depends on your deductible and whether it's met. She successfully stressed to her insurer that excision is preventative and diagnostic care that heads off an emergency hysterectomy or hospitalizations later; a framing worth borrowing.
Specialist and hospital were both in-network, so she owed only what remained of her $1,600 annual deductible.
Insurance covered it, in her words, no questions asked.

charges_note: hospital's itemized bill alone · hysterectomy + excision
paid_note: expected after adjustments and remaining bills
notes: April surgery. The $142k itemized hospital bill excludes still-pending bills from specialists, surgical assistants, anesthesia and pathology; after insurance adjustments she expects to owe around $10k-12k in total.
Free, but she notes finding a specialist is difficult and waitlists can run years.

Waited just under 3 years to see an endo specialist, then only about 2 months for surgery. No cost for the operation, but despite living in a big city she had to drive an hour and a half each way to a small rural hospital, and paid for drugs that weren't covered.

Years of dismissal from age 13 onward, then a 10-month wait for a gynecologist, an 11-month wait for the chronic pelvic pain clinic, and finally a surgery date just 2.5 months after the specialist found it. Working in a daycare, she says she'll take the wait times over a medical bill she could never repay.

Diagnosed very late, and only because she researched endo herself and pushed for a referral after a chance out-of-hours doctor suggested her stomach pain might be gynecological. Once she named endo, her doctors agreed it explained everything. At least, she notes, it was free.

paid_note: owed to the hospital after a laparoscopy
notes: Good PPO insurance, and her surgeon was the doctor who delivered her. Waits: 3 months for the appointment, 6 months for surgery, and still $6k owed after.
Went private. She then became critically ill, spent over a month in ICU and another 40 days in a public hospital needing dialysis, and paid nothing more for any of it.

paid_note: surgeon had a large gap fee · ≈ US$1,650
notes: Her last excision, private with insurance; most of her out-of-pocket was the surgeon's gap fee.

Her first and second surgeries were the identical procedure, surgical team, hospital and 4-5 hour duration, yet out-of-pocket jumped from AU$500 in May 2022 to AU$7,000 in September 2023, disclosed only the day before surgery. An emergency hysterectomy that November added AU$9,000.

paid_note: June 2023 · insurer covered about half; Medicare covered none · ≈ US$4,600
notes: Medicare wouldn't cover any of the surgery. The silver lining: with a formal endo diagnosis, her egg freezing (normally ~AU$11k per round) now costs her $0 between Medicare and her insurer.

provider: Mr. Shaheen Khazali · The Lister Hospital (HCA)
charges_note: robotic excision + partial vaginectomy + discoidectomy + 4 nights · ≈ US$17,800
paid_note: claimed via private medical insurance · ~£100 take-home meds outstanding
notes: Rates him a top surgeon people travel internationally to see. His initial consultation is £400, follow-ups less; MRI, ultrasounds and pathology bill separately. She notes UK private medical insurance covers treating chronic conditions when symptomatic but not routine monitoring, and offered to share her full cost paperwork with others. (Two comments from this patient combined.)

paid_note: ≈ AU$5k surgeon + ≈ AU$2k anaesthetist · zero hospital fees · ≈ US$4,600
notes: Private care with no hospital fees; her costs were the surgeon and anaesthetist.
Shared without further detail.

charges_note: billed to her private insurer for surgery + hospital stay · ≈ US$5,500
notes: Because she holds German private insurance she could see the bill: about €5,000 total, and her insurer was even refunded some of it later after an overcharge was found.
Her OBGYN operates only privately but could do the surgery straight away rather than after roughly a year in the public queue. Without private cover for gynecology, she paid the ~AU$5,000 herself.

charges_note: private hysterectomy + excision, by complexity · ≈ US$3,800-19,000
notes: On the NHS the same surgery is free with a 1-5 year wait.

paid_note: copay for her first lap, days away
notes: First laparoscopy with a specialist scheduled within days of the report.

paid_note: deductible for her lap, plus a few dollars of prescriptions
notes: Shared without further detail.
Qualifying required becoming too sick to work full time anymore. In her words: oof.

charges_val: full itemized bills aren't shown to Australian patients
charges_note: full itemized bills aren't shown to Australian patients
notes: Notes Australians usually quote what they paid, since they never see the US-style full bill. Her second surgery ended in ICU; insurance covered what she understands was a very expensive stay and she paid only about AU$1,000 for it. A colonoscopy to rule out GI causes cost her nearly AU$8k on top.

charges_note: total, as reported · hysterectomy + endo removal
notes: Shared without further detail.
Traveled abroad to Portugal for Stage IV surgery with an endo surgeon plus a general surgeon because her intestines were affected, including a 3-day hospital stay.

charges_note: what she knows people pay privately · ≈ US$12,000-18,000
notes: Counts herself lucky to have avoided the long waits; she knows others who have waited years or can't even get onto the waitlist. Finds six-figure US bills unfathomable next to local private prices.

paid_note: ≈ AU$300 initial consult + AU$100 anaesthetist review + AU$30 meds · ≈ US$280
notes: Fully public with no private insurance, yet with an excision-trained surgeon who listed her as category 2, giving a 2-month wait; all later appointments were bulk-billed. She offered to share her surgeon's name with other Victorians and will sing her praises forever.
The flat public rate applies to all surgeries. She waited about 3 months, slightly slowed by summer holidays.
Calls it horrid to shoulder a cost that high while sick.

charges_note: surgery alone, excluding anesthesia and everything else
notes: Shared in response to another six-figure bill: hers was higher.

charges_val: hospital stay ≈ AU$7,000, covered by insurance
charges_note: hospital stay ≈ AU$7,000, covered by insurance
paid_note: out of pocket for her chosen surgeon · ≈ US$3,300
notes: Public care would cost nothing but means a ~1-year wait with whichever doctor is on rotation. Her bronze-tier cover (~AU$150/month, bought individually, with tax incentives for average-and-up earners) paid the hospital stay while she paid the surgeon she chose.
Went private through workplace insurance because it was easier than joining the NHS waitlist.

charges_note: cash estimates she has collected for excision
paid_note: - her current in-network coverage would pay in full
notes: Points out that US prices vary wildly state to state since every hospital, physician and clinic sets its own fees, and every policy differs.

paid_note: her out-of-pocket max for the year
notes: Surprised by how much more the same surgery gets billed elsewhere.

charges_note: partial hysterectomy + endo removal + bowel resection · posted as "$500,00+", read here as $500,000+
notes: Keeps the bill hanging on her fridge and says, in her words, she's not paying it.
Fast-tracked because doctors suspected cancer; it turned out to be endo. Her insurer initially tried to deny the claim but covered it in the end.

One of the few complete itemizations shared: pathologist $972, pathology services $310, anesthesia $6,216, surgeon $2,706, radiology $371.70, and the one-night hospital stay $40,990.50. The facility, not the surgeon, was 80% of the bill.

paid_note: $500 hospital copay + 25% of the surgeon, anesthesiologist and pathology fees
notes: Lucked into a copay-only hospital fee because the hospital is her employer, which, as she puts it, is the least they can do.

paid_note: private, very experienced surgeon, incl. 2 nights · ≈ US$1,000
notes: Insurance covered the rest. The public system would have been $0, but with a long wait and possibly a less experienced surgeon.

paid_note: day-surgery fee plus the nights she stayed · ≈ US$220
notes: The money isn't the struggle; access is. Referrals to gynecology require persistence, the system asks you to try other treatments first, and she ultimately paid for a private gynecologist visit just to get dienogest (her personal miracle) prescribed. She agrees with cautious surgery gatekeeping in principle, but feels it goes too far sometimes.

charges_note: the private hospitals' prices for her 2 surgeries
notes: Without coverage, her two private-hospital surgeries would have cost $95k and $120k.

charges_note: typical private cost, which varies · ≈ US$1,100-1,650
notes: Shared without further detail.

charges_note: what her public insurer paid · ≈ US$4,400
notes: Shared without further detail.
Free, but with a 15-month surgery waitlist on top of the 6-8 months it took to even see a specialist.

charges_note: complex private excision (e.g. bowel involvement) · ≈ US$22,000
notes: A hysterectomy alone can be done publicly under insurance, but lesion excision, especially complicated cases involving the intestines, is very rarely done in public hospitals. Endo-specialist visits are typically uncovered too, at €100-150 including ultrasound.

charges_note: 1st: teaching hospital, bi-salp + excision · 2nd: hysterectomy + excision
paid_note: out-of-pocket max, then plan-year timing covered the second in full
notes: Her May 2023 surgery hit her out-of-pocket max; because her plan year ran April to March, the February 2024 surgery cost nothing. She wryly notes the much better surgeon billed around $30k where the teaching hospital had billed $80k+.

paid_note: Medicare covered 75%, private cover (~AU$160/month) the remaining 25%
notes: No trouble finding a specialist, and surgery happened within a few weeks at a private hospital; the public route would have meant a much longer wait.

paid_note: some carry extra insurance and pay nothing
notes: The catch is the referral (Überweisung): many doctors dismiss period pain as normal, and without a believing doctor you pay privately. Someone in her support group paid around €3,000 going without one.
Shared in solidarity with those who have to pay so much elsewhere.

charges_note: itemized bill · lap removing 2 cysts + Stage IV excision
notes: Shared without further detail.
Shared without further detail.
Had to rule the surgery out entirely; too expensive in her country. The kind of report this tracker exists to change.
Shared without further detail.

charges_note: expected cost of her hysterectomy, as written · ≈ US$2,500
notes: Her insured estimate for an upcoming hysterectomy.
Shared without further detail.

paid_note: private hospital, with health insurance · ≈ US$275
notes: Shared without further detail.
Asked whether laps are usually this expensive without coverage; with insurance her share was $853.

paid_note: $1,600 deductible met + 20% coinsurance to her in-network max
notes: Her plan started in April and she had already hit $3,000 of out-of-pocket costs before the lap bill even arrived, not counting recovery purchases like ice packs and a surgical pillow, plus $200 a month besides. (Two comments from this patient combined.)

charges_note: diagnostic lap + excision lap, 2nd still processing
notes: Her diagnostic lap billed at $59k cost her $3,000; the $118k excision claim was still working through insurance.

paid_note: day surgery + anaesthetist + post-op specialist appointments · ≈ US$330
notes: Elective through her own gyno in the private system; the public route would have been free.
Shared without further detail.

charges_note: average private cost she cites · ≈ US$12,700
notes: After moving to the US she was admitted to the ER and gyn ward for a ruptured cyst, no surgery involved, and the bill was $30,000 (she paid ~$300, hitting her out-of-pocket max). Her side-by-side of the two systems.
She raised her concerns with her OBGYN and handled scheduling and recovery on her own, at 31. (Two comments from this patient combined.)
Shared without further detail.

charges_val: billed amounts aren't disclosed to Australian patients
charges_note: billed amounts aren't disclosed to Australian patients
notes: Three completed laps plus one abandoned when she had an anaphylactic reaction to the anaesthetic during induction and was transferred to ICU. She learned a single bowel staple runs $675, and she needed several. Unable to get treatment through the public system, she took out loans she has never caught up on and remains thousands in debt on a disability pension. Private surgeons required the full cost upfront with no payment plans, her credit card has been maxed for years, and she pays a couple hundred dollars a month in premiums on top; credit and debt, she says, were the only way to afford endo care. (Two comments from this patient combined.)
Doesn't recall the pre-insurance figure.
Shared without further detail.
Waited close to 5 years from the day of referral to the day she was on the operating table.
Laparoscopic endo ablation.
Pre-surgery estimate; she has no idea yet what insurance will cover.
Shared without further detail.

charges_note: insurer paid hospital, anesthesia, pathology + surgeon fee
paid_note: surgeon fee, on a new credit card
notes: UnitedHealthcare would only approve ablation-type surgery, writing there was no "medical necessity" for a true excision specialist, despite stage 4 deep infiltrating endo with bowel lesions and an ovary that had to be excised and reconstructed. She opened a credit card for the $15k surgeon fee and is still paying it off two years later.
Shared without further detail.
Shared without further detail.

paid_note: April surgery, no deductible met yet
notes: Notes her insurer is known for being difficult, and the early-plan-year timing, with nothing yet toward her deductible, worked against her.

Her insurer initially refused to cover it at all, arguing she had no official endo diagnosis before surgery, even though surgery is the only way to get one; her surgeon called and fought until it was covered as much as it was. She later had her next excision in Germany for €40 (see that report).

paid_note: excision + full hysterectomy + 4-night stay · €10/day, waivable for low income
notes: Same patient as the ~$10k US report, roughly a decade later. She found German post-op care significantly better and notes she pays less for insurance there than she did in the US.
It would have been free with the surgeon her insurer picked, but they only told her about that option two weeks before surgery, after she had done a mountain of research choosing her own.

charges_note: robotic diagnostic lap + excision, before insurance
notes: Shared without further detail.
A complication landed her in intensive care for three days; insurance covered all of it.

Stage 4 deep infiltrating endo on all her organs: bowel resection, appendectomy and gallbladder removal, with a week in hospital. Two years on, symptoms are returning and she plans to go through the public system next time to keep costs down.
Shared without further detail.

paid_note: on her parents' private cover · ≈ US$1,980
notes: Went private; she describes the public queue in blunt terms.
Shared without further detail.
Shared without further detail.
Her premium was about $100/month with tax credits in a low-income year.

paid_note: out-of-pocket max + out-of-network surgeon paid upfront
notes: Hospital in-network, surgeon out. She could have pivoted to an in-network surgeon but judged her specialist's experience worth the extra $3,000 upfront; surgery was a week away.
A second lap with a new doctor is coming, quoted at $10,000.

paid_note: $1,700 deductible, mostly met by pre-op appointments
notes: Shared without further detail.
Shared without further detail.
Roughly 2 lakh rupees for the surgery, with pre-op testing on top.

charges_note: hospital bill + outpatient meds and scans · ≈ US$2,000
notes: Stage 4 with fibroids: a single lap covering both myomectomy and excision.

After a long search she finally found a specialist for a lap and possible hysterectomy, then got this out-of-network quote her insurance won't touch; she came to the community asking whether it's normal.

paid_note: balance erased by hospital financial assistance
notes: Her $2,500 share on a payment plan proved unmanageable, so she applied for the hospital financial-assistance program and the entire balance was wiped based on her income.

charges_note: before insurance · bi-salp + endo check and spot removal
notes: Shared without further detail.

paid_note: $3,000 deductible, then 30% coinsurance to her max
notes: Her deductible had just reset when the approval came through.
Her advice: the bill will definitely exceed your deductible, so the number to check is your out-of-pocket max. After hitting hers, a later ER trip for a kidney stone cost nothing.
Good insurance, but still a chunk of money for a broke college student.

paid_note: partly rebated, but only after paying in full · ≈ US$3,960
notes: Freshly out of a PhD, she says she couldn't have managed it without a financially stable partner. Her final invoice exceeded the quote because surgery ran longer in theatre, and the rebate arrives only once everything is paid out of pocket. Immensely relieved to have a diagnosis.

paid_note: post-op meds CA$160 + IUD CA$420 + transport · ≈ US$440
notes: The surgery itself is free; her costs are meds, an IUD and getting there, with work insurance likely reimbursing CA$200-300. She first asked for help at 15 and was diagnosed at 33, and calls Canada's endo support terrible even when the price is right.

paid_note: appointments, tests, surgery and follow-ups, all-in · ≈ US$1,980
notes: Shared without further detail.

charges_note: 3-hour surgery, no overnight: excision, pathology, meds
notes: Shared without further detail.
Shared without further detail.

paid_note: was already close to her deductible
notes: Points out costs vary with location, treatment, insurance, and each doctor and hospital setting its own rates.

charges_note: estimated private cost for her longer stay · ≈ US$16,500
notes: Shared without further detail.

charges_note: all tests + surgery, per her claims, some still pending
paid_note: no final bill yet; looks like little to nothing
notes: Shared without further detail.
Shared without further detail.

paid_note: Croatian patient, traveled for a specialist
notes: At home in Croatia the surgery is free in public hospitals or roughly $2,000-3,000 private; she chose a Bucharest specialist instead, and finds US prices depressing.

charges_note: insurer's contracted rate closer to $10,000 · no overnight
notes: Having hit the max, she deliberately crammed in every other appointment she could before the plan year reset.

charges_note: what it costs without insurance, per her searching · ≈ US$3,300-5,500
notes: With statutory insurance they simply scan your card at the hospital; you never even find out what it cost.
Shared without further detail.
Shared without further detail.
Shared without further detail.

paid_note: emergency lap + cystectomy · 2-night stay, all imaging included
notes: An emergency lap and cystectomy for a ruptured endometrioma in what she calls a very nice public hospital, covering surgery, ward, X-ray, ultrasounds and CT scans, and she isn't even a permanent resident.
No insurance; it took months of paperwork, but the hospital brought the $70k bill down to under $1,000.
Her bronze cover paid the entire hospital bill (about AU$6,000) plus small contributions to other fees.

charges_note: what the 2nd would have cost uninsured · ≈ US$19,800
paid_note: 1st fully out of pocket, 2nd with insurance · ≈ US$12,540
notes: Her first surgery came entirely out of pocket; by the second she had cover and paid a fraction.
Her take on the trade: US patients pay, but can get surgery whenever needed with no wait list.

paid_note: fully covered; everyone there is insured
notes: She had to look the price up, because she never saw a bill.
After the insurance adjustment, she believes her share ended up larger than what the insurer itself actually paid. Rough, she says, but worth the pain relief.

charges_note: billed in 2020, then negotiated down by the insurer
notes: The hospital billed $55k; the insurer negotiated the real price to $14,000, a $41,000 markup that never stuck. Parking at the hospital ramp, meanwhile, was $12 an hour.
Shared without further detail.
Her local NHS waitlist was 3+ years, under a surgeon she found misinformed and dismissive about endometriosis, so she went private.
Shared without further detail.
Shared without further detail.
Shared without further detail.

charges_note: hospital & 1 night + surgeon + pathologist + anesthesia team
notes: A month post-op from robotic stage 4 debulking with a 12cm endometrioma removed and a unilateral salpingectomy; every line item was still working through her private insurance.

charges_note: hospital, anesthesiologist, blood work and surgeon
notes: Shared without further detail.

paid_note: self-pay rate despite having insurance, + separately billed anesthesia
notes: On a monthly payment plan that leaves her barely able to afford her other bills.
Shared without further detail.
Shared without further detail.
Shared without further detail.
The charge was ~$50k, but the insurer's contracted payment was only $4,000-5,000: one of the clearest looks in this tracker at how far billed prices sit from real ones.

charges_note: lap + excision + D&C + appendectomy + HSG + IUD removal + cyst drainage
paid_note: everything from pre-op through both post-ops
notes: August 2022 surgery.
Free, which mattered: she hasn't been able to work in about two years.
Public insurance there makes it practically free, but she notes qualifying is hard, especially as an immigrant.

charges_note: what her private cover paid for surgery + stay · ≈ US$5,940
notes: Shared without further detail.

charges_note: typical private lap price she has seen · ≈ US$12,700
notes: Free on the NHS, but she went private through her employer's insurance.
A colorectal surgeon attended too. She was shocked by the amount, and disheartened to still be in the same pain.

paid_note: insurer refused coverage as a "pre-existing condition" · ≈ US$7,700
notes: Publicly it usually costs under €500 or nothing, if you can endure the waiting lists; her insurer's pre-existing-condition refusal left her paying privately.
Shared without further detail.

paid_note: two ~$35 fees: consult + surgery · ~3 years ago
notes: Norway caps annual out-of-pocket health costs around $300; past the cap, everything is free for the rest of the year.

Her Californian insurer covered nothing, reasoning that because no billing code distinguishes excision from ablation, going out of network for his specific surgery was "seemingly unnecessary", even though it could not be done locally. She still highly recommends him.
Traveled from California to Oregon for her; worth every penny, in her words.

paid_note: insurance share + pre-surgery deposit to the doctor
notes: Shared without further detail.

charges_note: one year: 2 laps (one with a gyn oncologist), pathology ×2, CT
notes: A public-school teacher whose $160/month plan for two covered everything past the initial $3,500. Low pay, she notes, but the healthcare delivered.

charges_note: diagnostic lap; a suspected giant cyst turned out to be endo
notes: Shared without further detail.
Full hysterectomy plus lap; no deposit was required beforehand.

paid_note: max already met from an earlier surgery; else $3,100
notes: She points out the $0 excludes monthly premiums and the previous year of care leading up to surgery.

paid_note: was liable for $156,000 at one point; most later written off
notes: Three laps, two of them excisions. At the worst point she was on the hook for $156k before most of it was written off.
Her excision surgeon is in network, which she knows is rare: her own obgyn happens to be MIGS- and excision-trained.

charges_note: basic exploratory lap → complex multi-surgeon excision · ≈ US$4,400-33,000
notes: Cost tracks complexity, surgeon skill and hospital. A basic exploratory lap by an endo-experienced gyn runs about £3,500; rectovaginal or bowel-resection excision with multiple specialised surgeons more like £26,000. Most surgeons want a specialist ultrasound (~£300) first, for planning and an accurate quote.

charges_note: simple lap; excision double or more
notes: From a quick search: UK private clinics openly display their prices online, with simple laps under £4k and excision roughly double or more.

charges_note: estimated add-on fee for a separate bowel surgeon, not guaranteed
notes: Given to a patient weighing out-of-network surgery with a ~20% chance of needing a bowel resection mid-lap, which would be performed by a different bowel specialist at extra cost.

paid_note: excludes imaging, pre/post-op appointments and physio
notes: The bowel resection is by far the more expensive part, she notes. A second surgery is scheduled three months later, on top. (Two comments from this patient combined.)

charges_note: emailed estimate: surgery + hospital + hotel · ≈ US$27,500
notes: An American living near Naples got this rough estimate from ESSI's Salerno location while hunting for excision options; it tracks closely with the ~$28k ESSI figure reported in New York elsewhere in this tracker. She'd be paying out of pocket and is searching for alternatives in Italy.

paid_note: stage 4, incl. bowel resection by a 2nd surgeon + 3-4 day stay · ≈ US$8,000
notes: A Canadian patient's hospital bill for stage 4 surgery in Romania, shared in disbelief at six-figure quotes elsewhere.
Shared without further detail.

Saved on scans because her consultant could reuse her NHS imaging; she calls it the best money she has ever spent, and says she would have used a credit card or loan if needed, though she does not want to encourage debt. (Two comments from this patient combined.)
Shared ahead of surgery.
Shared in reaction to a far larger bill.
Suspects providers bill insurers as much as they can, and is glad to still be on her parents' plan.

charges_note: stage 4: kissing ovaries, 2 endometriomas, appendectomy, bowel resection, ovary suspension
notes: The bowel resection was performed by a second surgeon, and her ovaries had to be suspended.

paid_note: 1st + 2nd surgeries; a somewhat cheaper 3rd on top · ≈ US$24,000+
notes: Three surgeries; the third cost a bit less than the second.
Shared without further detail.

Her surgeon's flat cash fee is $15,000, yet the insurance-billed figure topped $500k, which she finds incomprehensible. Details resemble another $400k-billed / $15k-cash report in this tracker; possibly the same surgery shared twice.
No idea why, in her words; she calls it actually insane.

paid_note: hysterectomy · OON surgeon fee, despite having insurance
notes: Her surgeon stopped taking insurance after her first surgery; she trusted them enough to stay and pay the fee herself.
Shared in reaction to a far larger bill.
Shared with astonishment that even bigger numbers exist.
Shared without further detail.
In her words: cries in Californian.
Gathered while comparison-shopping specialists; the same patient priced Dr. Sinervo too (next report). It matches the $3,500 Vidali fee another patient in this tracker reports actually paying.
The same comparison shopper's CEC quote: the self-pay surgeon rate runs lower than the with-insurance rate, and neither includes what you will owe the hospital.
Shared with relief; she had assumed a top center's fee would run $30k+.

paid_note: for what was sold as excision
notes: Paid nearly $3,000 for excision only to learn ablation was used; the endometriomas came back, in her words, with a friend. A leftover $1,800 bill from the 2023 lap surfaced the weekend before her second lap date, which she feels should be forgiven since the first one failed.
Shared without further detail.
Wonders why US patients are left paying figures like that themselves.

charges_val: still being settled with the insurer
charges_note: still being settled with the insurer
notes: Interviewed several surgeons specifically to find one who only does excision, after hearing others' ablation stories.

charges_note: surgeon + hospital fees her insurance will not cover
notes: A well-known excision specialist who is luckily local to her; she is wrestling with paying over $20k for a diagnostic lap to look for something unconfirmed, over pain she has been gaslit about for years.

charges_note: typical all-in private excision with a specialist · ≈ US$10,200-12,700
notes: She notes plenty of people travel for it, including US to UK, precisely because the total can undercut a US bill.

paid_note: the research hospital's own out-of-pocket maximum
notes: Her research hospital caps what any patient pays; after meeting its $3,500 max she has not owed a single copay there since, including for her PT and pain management.

paid_note: PPO through her employer, Urban Outfitters
notes: Shared without further detail.
Shared without further detail.

charges_note: 7.5-hour surgery + overnight, per the insurance note
notes: A 2015 four-specialist surgery: endo lead, ob/gyn assistant, urologist and colon specialist, with extensive excision, removal of her cervix, uterus and part of her vaginal wall, and part of her left ureter removed and reattached to the kidney. Being all in one system, she says the paperwork and specialist coordination happened without a hitch.

charges_note: the out-of-pocket range she keeps encountering
notes: Searching NYC for excision, she has yet to find a surgeon who accepts insurance, hers or anyone's. Dead set on excision, she does not have $5-20k, and came asking for advice and recommendations.
Shared without further detail.

paid_note: her 20% share · $700 upfront, rest on a monthly plan
notes: A year on, the payment plan is still running. The joys, she says, of living in the USA.

paid_note: after meeting her deductible, minus a few hundred via a hospital grant
notes: Calling hospital billing and explaining her situation (broke, no job, surgery as a hail mary) got her onto a hospital grant that knocked a few hundred off. She also points to community endo groups, like one in Dallas-Fort Worth, that fund free or very low-cost surgeries through donations, and urges people not to give up: the random resources exist.

charges_val: estimates were nearly impossible to get
charges_note: estimates were nearly impossible to get
notes: Between hospital departments and her insurer, no one could give her a real estimate, so she budgeted for the maximum she could possibly be billed, which worked out.
Expects more bills to come in after the fact.
Free, she says, only because of her government insurance (she calls it Medicare, for low-income people).
Shared without further detail.
Her first surgery, a $1,000 ablation, did not help at all; the $5,000 was the do-over.

paid_note: out of pocket, private system · ≈ US$3,300
notes: Shared without further detail.

paid_note: insurance paid for all of it, luckily
notes: Shared without further detail.
And she is, in her words, worse off than before surgery.

charges_note: billed for surgery + 1 night; the hospital quoted $16,000 that same morning
paid_note: out-of-network costs; in-network deductible already met
notes: Her budgeting advice: expect to pay your entire in-network deductible plus at least some out-of-network, since someone like the anesthesiologist can turn out to be out of network.
Shared without further detail.

charges_note: the private route she was pointed to · ≈ US$7,600-8,900
notes: Her NHS doctor told her there was nothing he could do because her endo sounded too extensive for him, with no other specialist in reasonable distance, and pointed her private; she cannot raise the money and describes feeling abandoned and hopeless. Reports like this are why this tracker exists.
Super stressed about the cost: she does not have $1,000 to her name.

paid_note: required upfront before they would operate · ≈ US$3,300
notes: Payment in full was demanded before surgery would go ahead.

paid_note: the unmet rest of her $6,000 deductible, 4 years ago
notes: The hospital set up a 0% payment plan, any amount per month for as long as needed. Her advice: a plan signals you intend to pay, and billing offices will likely keep working with you.
Her verdict on seeing lower figures: dang, I must have got a bad deal.

charges_note: quoted range depending on what's required · ≈ US$14,000-33,000
notes: Cited as an example of how expensive the top accredited centres run.
Quoted in a UK private-care discussion; she was advised she may need more than one surgery, and notes others get quoted both less and more depending on what they need.

paid_note: November 2020 · surgery only, excl. MRI, image review and consults · ≈ US$12,700
notes: Shared without further detail.

charges_note: surgeon billed to out-of-network benefits + hospital billed in-network
paid_note: ≈ $10,000 to the surgeon + $4,000 in-network out-of-pocket max
notes: Her specialist takes no insurance: she charged about $7k for the excision while billing $100k to out-of-network benefits, which paid her only a few thousand. The in-network hospital billed $54k but was capped at the $4k max. She notes an HMO would have no out-of-network benefits at all, making even partial reimbursement impossible.
Her advice to the uninsured: apply for Medicaid, and ask the doctor to submit a pre-authorization so you learn the real price in advance, the same way you would get a wisdom-tooth estimate.

paid_note: after her employer more than doubled out-of-pocket costs
notes: Her workplace changed plans right before surgery, more than doubling what she will owe; her partner and parents are helping, but she feels defeated.

paid_note: this year's out-of-pocket max, up from $6,000 last year
notes: With surgery scheduled for May, at least everything after it will be, in her words, free.

paid_note: paid ahead of her 3rd endo surgery
notes: At 29, she is struggling with anger at what the illness costs: money, sanity, time with friends, and the vacations and life-building the pain and bills keep taking away.

paid_note: out-of-pocket max, hit by June; $1,400 deductible gone by mid-January
notes: The usual story, she says, along with using all of her PTO on surgery and recovery.

paid_note: out-of-pocket max, on a 2-year payment plan
notes: Thank gosh for payment plans, she says. For two years, she guesses.

paid_note: last July’s lap, after her deductible; still paying it off
notes: Her pre-hysterectomy colonoscopy was covered as preventative right up until they found and removed a polyp, at which point a $487 bill arrived because it was, quote, no longer preventative. Removing a cancer risk, she points out, is exactly what preventative means.

paid_note: two surgeries in 2020, after an 11-month appeal
notes: Mid-surgery, endo had rotted her appendix and forced an emergency appendectomy, which her insurer refused to pay because the appendix surgeon was not pre-approved (for an emergency). She won after 11 months by pulling in her employer's HR, then still owed $6k because the hospital sat outside her plan's top tier; her second, fully covered surgery still cost about $2,500. Her conclusion: health insurance is a scam.
Medical costs, she says, are downright insane.
Hoping the anesthesiologist bills nothing since her deductible is met.

paid_note: the year of bills leading to diagnosis + surgery
notes: A hard pill to swallow, she says, with symptoms still there four months post-op.
On top of roughly $175 a month across her health and other insurances; she is angry her health has to cost this much.

charges_val: her family paid $50,000 in care this year
charges_note: her family paid $50,000 in care this year
paid_note: out-of-network specialist, 2nd excision in under a year
notes: Her first surgeon turned out not to have excised at all, just removed some pieces for testing and declared it all gone; she was back to square one within 3 months. A true endo specialist is redoing it. With dual UK citizenship, she is seriously weighing a move before aging off her parents' plan at 26.

charges_note: ≈ $3k surgeon + ≈ $5k OR and drugs + ≈ $46k overnight stay; reduced on appeal
notes: The bill nearly went to collections while she fought it; with her mom's help on the phones, her insurance appeal cut $54k to $14k. It baffles her that the vanished $40,000 was never going to the nurses who actually cared for her all night. Closely resembles the $55,000 → $14,000 report elsewhere in this tracker; possibly the same surgery shared twice.
Shared without further detail.
Not in the States, she notes.

charges_note: lap myomectomy + endo excision, without insurance
notes: Shared without further detail.

paid_note: AU$3,500 + AU$500 excess, with private cover · ≈ US$2,640
notes: Shared without further detail.
Shared without further detail.

paid_note: chose her own doctor for comfort
notes: Her plan added a surgery-benefit program (Surgery Plus) in 2023 that would have made the operation completely free, with travel and housing covered if no local provider participates; she paid the $4,000 anyway to stay with the doctor she trusted, and shares it so others check whether their plan hides something similar.

charges_note: the surgeon's price, despite both her maxes being met
notes: After a month-long hospitalization she had met her out-of-pocket max for both in- and out-of-network providers this year and assumed the diagnostic lap would be covered; the surgeon's office called to say the $12,000 was still on her. In-network surgeons exist near her, but she is scared to hand her pain to a non-expert.

charges_note: charged once insurance was involved; the cash quote had been $25,000
notes: Quoted $6,000 at first, then $25,000 for excision, she took a job she hates specifically for its insurance; the same surgery then billed at $90,000 because insurance was paying. The whole thing, she says, is crazy.

charges_note: hospital stay, covered after a hard-fought appeal
notes: Twelve weeks out from excision, hysterectomy and appendectomy. She found her specialist through the Nancy's Nook Facebook group, which also tracks which excision surgeons accept insurance; winning coverage of the $75k hospital stay took bringing in a patient advocate, which she says helped tremendously.

paid_note: paid in full by her dad, the day before surgery
notes: Even with what she calls really great job insurance, affording it was hard; her father stepped in the day before, after a year in which other bills had already drained her.

charges_note: the hospital's estimate; unclear if anesthesia etc. is included
notes: Preparing for her first lap and trying to set expectations; answers she had seen ranged $500 to $5,000.

provider: Not shared · MIGS specialist, outpatient surgical center
notes: She got real numbers only by phoning the surgical center directly, which warned that the surgical fee, pathology and anesthesia would each bill separately; the surgeon could quote her own fee, but pathology and anesthesia were nearly impossible to pin down in advance.
Her advice: your deductible and maximum are the numbers that decide it; once you hit them, insurance covers the rest.
Shared in reaction to lower figures.

paid_note: over the course of a year · ~$500 deductible, good out-of-pocket max
notes: Expects her next lap to land around the same under her current plan.
They took wonderful care of her and she has felt great since recovery; no regrets spending the money. Lands right beside the ~$3,000 UCSF estimate reported above.

provider: Dr. Andrea Vidali · Hoboken University Hospital
paid_note: Vidali's surgical fee; the in-network hospital cost nothing extra
notes: Matches the $3,500 Vidali fee quote reported elsewhere in this tracker.

paid_note: under her previous Kaiser plan; new work plan unknown
notes: Anxious about what her new employer plan will charge; the old number was more than she makes in a month.

paid_note: her last surgery; the next is out of network
notes: Has exhausted her in-network options and is crowdfunding the next surgery through GoFundMe. It's insane, she says.

paid_note: paid upfront; receipts submitted toward her deductible
notes: Chose a specialist to be sure it was done right the first time rather than risk a redo.
Cedars-Sinai has an online tool that estimates your cost from your insurance details, and what she calls a pretty generous financial-aid program she hopes will trim the final bill.
A very wide range with a lot of factors, she notes: location, insurance and more.

paid_note: 1st surgery; the 2nd that year was free after her deductible
notes: Shared without further detail.
Chose a private clinic, which made it a little more expensive, but the country still paid for most of it.
Shared without further detail.
Endo was found and excised; her insurer denied it anyway.

paid_note: hospital + surgeon, against her unmet $1,500 deductible; insurance then covered 80%
notes: Shared without further detail.
